evidence_quotes.quote: actions that manipulate their behaviour such as dark patterns.

paragraphs.text: Student of the Jean Monnet Modules project “DERECHO”: ‘Defense of European Rights and Excellence for Consumers HHarmonisation’, No 101239437. Project financed by the European Union. Jean Monnet Actions in the field of Higher Education: Modules. Call for tenders ERASMUS-JMO-2025-HEI-TCH-RSCH. Personally, I consider that the main problem lies in the low awareness and insufficient understanding of consumers about the digital space. There is a clear lack of knowledge, at global level, that most people have about the online world. Despite the fact that the adult generation is commonly stripped of being the main subjects lacking comprehensive skills with regard to new technologies. I consider that this case concerns both the adult and the most recent generations, children, and even affects the generations to come. Due to its broad scope, changes are needed now to be able to create a more secure system for a future. As I have already stated, in my view, the key action to be taken in every Member State of the European Union is to promote education and training in the digital field. If a citizen is trained in this matter, he or she can know how to use it and, above all, how to deal with the harm it causes. This is the same as when we learn a subject at school. In this way, one important step to take would be to implement digital space subjects in schools, as well as technological training courses in jobs and, for the most adults, conferences or dynamic activities that facilitate a better understanding of the dangers of the online world. These activities do not have to be dense, it is sufficient for users to be aware of the most present dangers such as hackers or cyber-attacks (ramsonware, phising, etc.). In this context, and closer to the project, it would be useful to disseminate or promote greater knowledge about online shopping, which I consider to be one of the most widespread daily problems. It is therefore necessary to create new legislation as to ensure that consumers have an effective understanding of it, we must ensure that they are aware of their rights to online shopping and, even more so, how to deal with actions that manipulate their behaviour such as dark patterns. In relation to the latter, as already discussed in a proposal from the consumer’s point of view, it is now worth talking what measures should be taken by offerors or businesses using new technologies to sell their product. In my view, businesses should take a closer initiative to consumers when designing their websites, seeking to avoid misleading advertising that influences consumer behaviour and eliminating false prices. Although they wish to obtain the best possible value for money, the means they are using are not appropriate, but it would be fair for them to present a clear picture of their products, without inputs seeking to disrupt the consumer’s decision such as changing the size, colour, structure or analogues of texts and images in order to take advantage of those consumers with less rational understanding, such as minors. However, action is needed from the point of view of companies as well as education institutions or, at least, public institutions, in order to increase citizens’ training in the digital world.
